list.name: workload
list.label: Workload
list.description: Container name, used as the stable per-workload key. cAdvisor and kube-state-metrics carry no `service`/`app` label, so the old `service` variable could not filter the cgroup/OOM panels; `container` is the one label common to cAdvisor, KSM and the app metrics, and (unlike `pod`) it survives pod replacement. Sourced from cAdvisor so postgres/qdrant (which emit no app metrics) are still selectable.

panels.title: Container working set vs memory limit
panels.description: Per-workload cgroup working set (cAdvisor) against the configured memory limit, aggregated max-by-container so it is one continuous line per workload across pod replacement (a rolling deploy briefly runs two pods; `max by (container)` follows the live one instead of spawning a ghost series per pod name). The gap to the dashed limit line is OOM headroom; open this first during a memory incident.

panels.title: Process RSS by service (memprobe)
panels.description: process_vm_rss_bytes from the in-process memprobe. Aggregated max-by-service so a rolling deploy (two pods of one service briefly co-existing) stays one line instead of two identically-labelled ones. Filtered by $workload via the `container` label. Cross-check against the cAdvisor working set above; divergence points at page-cache vs anon RSS.

panels.title: Working set as % of limit
panels.description: max-by-container working set / limit (both from cAdvisor, identical labels, so the division is clean and replacement-proof). Sustained approach to 100% precedes an OOMKill.

targets.expr: 100 * max by (container) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="minerva", container=~"$workload", container!="", container!="POD"}) / max by (container) (container_spec_memory_limit_bytes{namespace="minerva", container=~"$workload", container!="", container!="POD"} > 0)

panels.title: OOMKilled containers (last terminated reason)
panels.description: kube_pod_container_status_last_terminated_reason filtered to OOMKilled, scoped by $workload. A row means that container's last exit was an OOM kill. Pod name is kept here on purpose; when chasing an OOM you want the exact (now-replaced) pod.

panels.title: Container restarts (last 1h)
panels.description: increase(kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total[1h]) summed by container. Plotting the *increase* rather than the raw cumulative counter is deliberate: the cumulative series resets to 0 every time a pod is replaced, so on a normal rolling deploy the old chart dropped to zero and looked like a metric glitch. The windowed increase is replacement-proof and reads as what you actually care about: is anything crash-looping right now. A clean deploy stays flat at ~0; a crash-looping pod climbs.

panels.title: Threads by service (memprobe)
panels.description: process_threads, max-by-service (replacement-proof). A runaway thread count is an independent leak signal that shows up before RSS does.

panels.title: Peak RSS (VmHWM) by service
panels.description: process_vm_hwm_bytes: the high-water mark of RSS since process start, so it resets when a pod is replaced. max-by-service keeps it on one continuous line through a rolling deploy (it tracks whichever pod has the higher HWM, i.e. the old one until it ages out). The startup spike (embedder benchmark warmup) shows here even after steady-state RSS settles back down.
